---
output: github_document
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.path = "man/figures/README-",
  out.width = "100%"
)
```

# sybil

<!-- badges: start -->
[![Lifecycle: experimental](https://img.shields.io/badge/lifecycle-experimental-orange.svg)](https://lifecycle.r-lib.org/articles/stages.html#experimental)
[![R-CMD-check](https://github.com/mattwarkentin/sybil/actions/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/mattwarkentin/sybil/actions/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml)
<!-- badges: end -->

The goal of `sybil` is to provide an R interface to the Sybil lung cancer risk prediction model. Sybil is a deep learning model to predict future risk of lung cancer using low-dose computed tomography (LDCT) images. The publication for Sybil can be found [here](https://ascopubs.org/doi/10.1200/JCO.22.01345). This package is a wrapper around the Python implementation of [Sybil](https://github.com/reginabarzilaygroup/Sybil). 

This package is make possible by [`reticulate`](https://rstudio.github.io/reticulate/index.html), which simplifies the installation and management of Python dependencies for R users. Under the hood, `reticulate` uses [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) to rapidly install all required Python dependencies into an ephemeral virtual environment.

## Installation

You can install the development version of `sybil` from [GitHub](https://github.com/mattwarkentin/sybil) with:

``` r
# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("mattwarkentin/sybil")
```

## Usage

Once installed, you can load the package with:

```r
library(sybil)
```

Next, we provide some convenience functions to simplify identifying the set of
DICOM images that form a specific series that you want to estimate the time-dependent lung cancer risk. For example:

```r
series_ids <- find_series_in_dir("/path/to/images")
dicoms <- find_dicoms_in_series("/path/to/images", series_ids[[1]])
```

Next, you can load a Series:

```r
series <- Series(dicoms)
```

See `?Series` for more information on other arguments that can be passed to `Series()`.

Next, you can load a Sybil model using `Sybil()`. If this is your first time calling this function, it will need to download the weights for the Sybil model and may take some time. Subsequent calls to `Sybil()` will be considerably faster due to caching of the weights.

```r
# Default uses "sybil_ensemble"
model <- Sybil()
```

See `?Sybil` for more information.

Finally, you can predict lung cancer risk for the series:

```r
result <- predict(model, series)
result
```

In the above code, `result` is an [`S7`](https://rconsortium.github.io/S7/) object with two properties, `@scores` and `@attentions`. `@scores` is a list of predictions for each series. `@attentions` will either be an empty list, if `return_attentions = FALSE`, or a list of attention maps for each series.

## Citation

If you use this package in your work, please cite the Sybil paper:

```
@article{mikhael2023sybil,
  title={Sybil: a validated deep learning model to predict future lung cancer risk from a single low-dose chest computed tomography},
  author={Mikhael, Peter G and Wohlwend, Jeremy and Yala, Adam and Karstens, Ludvig and Xiang, Justin and Takigami, Angelo K and Bourgouin, Patrick P and Chan, PuiYee and Mrah, Sofiane and Amayri, Wael and Juan, Yu-Hsiang and Yang, Cheng-Ta and Wan, Yung-Liang and Lin, Gigin and Sequist, Lecia V and Fintelmann, Florian J. and Barzilay, Regina},
  journal={Journal of Clinical Oncology},
  pages={JCO--22},
  year={2023},
  publisher={Wolters Kluwer Health}
}
```

## Code of Conduct

Please note that the sybil project is released with a [Contributor Code of Conduct](https://contributor-covenant.org/version/2/1/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.html). By contributing to this project, you agree to abide by its terms.
